title: Case Statements 🗄️
description: Write cleaner code when you have many different conditions to check.

slides.title: Too Many Else-Ifs 🗑️
slides.content:
If you have 10 different possibilities, chaining `elsif` 10 times becomes tedious and difficult to read.

For these situations, Ruby provides the **Case Statement**.

slides.title: The Case Setup
slides.content:
You provide the `case` keyword followed by the variable you want to test.
Then, you provide `when` clauses for your potential matches.

```ruby
case day
when "Monday"
  puts "Start of the week"
when "Friday"
  puts "End of the week"
else
  puts "Just another day"
end
```

slides.title: Case in Action
slides.content: Instead of writing `if grade == "A"... elsif grade == "B"...`, a case statement makes it much cleaner.
slides.codeSnippet:
grade = "B"

case grade
when "A"
  puts "Fantastic job!"
when "B"
  puts "Great work!"
when "C"
  puts "You passed."
else
  puts "You failed."
end

slides.title: Quick Check 🧠
slides.content: How do you provide the options in a case statement?

quizData.question: Which keyword is used inside a `case` block to outline the different potential matches?
quizData.explanation: You use `case [variable]` followed by multiple `when [match]` clauses.

slides.title: Then Syntactic Sugar 🍬
slides.content:
If you want to keep your Case Statement extremely compact, you can use the `then` keyword to put everything on one line!

```ruby
case letter
when "A" then puts "Apple"
when "B" then puts "Banana"
end
```

quizData.question: What happens if absolutely ZERO strings match any of your `when` clauses?

options.text: The program throws a critical error.
It checks if there is an `else` block to run, otherwise it does nothing.
It runs the very first `when` block by default.

quizData.explanation: You can optionally define an `else` block at the bottom of a case statement to act as a catch-all.
